data.title: Western Midstream: Why I Am Buying This 9% Yield Hand Over Fist
data.description:
Western Midstream Partners (NYSE:WES) is a rapidly growing pipeline operator with an expanding asset footprint. WES recently announced the acquisition of Aris Water Solutions which is a premier water infrastructure platform in the Delaware Basin that enhances WES's asset base and that it expected to be accretive to free cash flow next year. I like the firm's focus on pipeline acquisitions, and with the deal estimated to be FCF accretive next year, WES is in a great spot to raise its distribution. Further, with an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of only 9.0X, the valuation and risk profile also support an investment in the pipeline company, in my opinion.

Previous rating

I previously rated units of WES a strong buy -- Impressive Value Proposition -- because I like the midstream platform's Pathfinder pipeline expansion. The Pathfinder pipeline is a 42-mile-long pipeline in New Mexico and Texas and is set to add incremental cash flow to the firm's business. The recent acquisition in the Delaware Basin diversified the firm's customer base and also limits its dependence on the natural gas business. I also like that Western Midstream Partners’ units are very affordable based off of an EBITDA basis and I see attractive long term growth prospects for the distribution.

Strong core business proposition in the midstream market

With energy needs growing and Data Centers being a key driver of energy demand going forward, WES is set to benefit from favorable long term business tailwinds. The pipeline operator has a strong asset footprint in the Permian Basin which connects the shale oil and gas company to important consumer end-markets as well as export hubs at the Gulf Coast. The midstream platform gets paid fees for the transport of energy raw materials, such as natural gas and natural gas liquids, crude oil, produced water and condensate.

WES owns a portfolio of natural gas pipelines, crude oil/NGL pipelines, gathering systems as well as processing facilities that make the company a crucial link between producers (upstream) and refineries (downstream). With energy demand expected to grow in the years ahead, and Western Midstream Partners signing long term fee-based delivery contracts with its producer clients, I believe WES has the potential to be a long term winner in the distribution business... especially since the midstream platform relies on acquisitions to grow its asset footprint.

In the second-quarter, Western Midstream Partners benefited from consistent results in its core business: the firm's operating cash flow was reported at $564M, showing 6% Q/Q growth, mainly due to higher natural gas and crude oil/NGL throughput volumes.

WES agreed in August to acquire Aris Water Solutions in an equity-and-cash deal worth $1.5B that includes produced-water handling and recycling capacity. The breakdown of the transaction shows that the following assets will migrate over to the control of Western Midstream:

  • 790 miles of produced-water pipeline,
  • 1,800 MBbls/d of produced-water handling capacity,
  • 1,400 MBbls/d of water recycling capacity, and
  • 625,000 dedicated acres from investment grade counterparties

The transaction is important because it helps WES to grow its non-natural gas business, diversify its customer base and add to its free cash flow. The share of produced water, as a percentage of estimated FY 2025 EBITDA, is expected to rise from 10% (pre-deal) to 16% (post-deal). This implies that natural gas will become slightly less important, although this energy source will still remain dominant in the midstream company's portfolio.

WES has said that it expects the deal to be accretive to free cash flow next year (FY 2026) which implies potential for a generous distribution increase next year. The energy enterprise has so far achieved, with the help of past acquisitions, an impressive capital return history, especially as far as the distribution is concerned. This year, the MLP raised its distribution, on an annual level, ~14% year-over-year to $3.64/unit. I expect at similar growth rate next year when the midstream platform enjoys the full effect of the Aris Water Solutions acquisition.

Western Midstream’s valuation

Units of Western Midstream Partners are currently trading at a forward EBITDA multiplier of 8.9X which is 12% below the industry group average enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 10.1X. For comparison, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) which is one of the largest midstream platforms in North America that also heavily relies on acquisitions to boost its EBITDA and distributable cash flow, is trading at a forward enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 10.1X. Kinder Morgan (KMI) and ONEOK (OKE) are priced at forward EBITDA multipliers of 11.5X and 9.7X.

In my opinion, WES could revalue to the industry group average enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 10.1X (or higher) if the firm can successfully integrate Aris Water Solutions and use the acquisition as a lever to incrementally add to its free cash flow next year. A 10.1X EBITDA multiplier calculates to a fair value for the midstream energy's units of $44/unit, implying about 13% upside revaluation potential. Additionally, income investors get to capture a 9.3% yield here, plus the distribution is likely to go up next year by a double-digit percentage as well.

Risks with Western Midstream

The biggest risk for Western Midstream Partners would be a potential slowdown in CapEx spending and production activity in the Delaware Basin, which is key to the company's growth strategy and which accounts for the majority of the firm’s EBITDA. What would change my mind about WES is if the midstream platform were to slow its distribution growth or failed to capture its expected incremental FCF potential from the Aris acquisition.

Final thoughts

Western Midstream Partners is a top distribution play for dividend investors that look for a steadily expanding midstream platform with an aggressive hunger for new pipeline acquisitions in its core market. The energy enterprise just spent $1.5B on the acquisition of Aris Water Solutions, which is set to expand the firm's operations in the important Delaware Basin, improve its revenue diversification, and also help broaden its customer base. WES has a convincing record in growing its distribution, and with the Aris Water Solutions deal expected to add FCF next year, the odds are in favor of another double-digit percentage increase in the payout in FY 2026. If you are looking for a durable and growing distribution stream, delivered by a dependable midstream platform, Western Midstream Partners may fit your investment criteria.

data.title: Scale, Scarcity, Strategy: 3 Faces Of Technological Power
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA & Samuel Rines

Oracle: Transformation at Scale

Every so often, a corporate earnings report delivers a number so overwhelming that it erases all the usual debates about revenue guidance, margin compression or quarterly beats relative to analyst expectations. For Oracle (ORCL), that number was $332 billion in bookings in a single quarter.1

This was not incremental growth. It was the largest bookings number ever reported in enterprise software, so large that it transformed how the market and policymakers must view Oracle. With contracts stretching up to a decade, including one annualizing at $30 billion, Oracle was no longer just a software vendor.2 It had become a graphics processing unit (GPU) data center operator of geopolitical consequence.

Ironically, Oracle missed on several traditional measures: revenue growth underwhelmed, cloud infrastructure growth lagged peers, and earnings per share (EPS) fell below consensus. But the bookings overwhelmed all else.3 The narrative had shifted. Oracle was no longer defined by quarterly comparisons to Amazon Web Services (AWS) or Azure. It was defining a new industrial category altogether.

This is theater at its highest form: the ability to redirect the entire conversation with a single figure. Oracle has seized control of the compute narrative, positioning itself as custodian of the most valuable resource of the AI era, GPU capacity.

The Stargate Project as Prelude

The company's Stargate project,4 once seen as a curiosity, now looks like a rehearsal. By collaborating on mega-scale data centers, Oracle tested its ability to think like an infrastructure operator rather than a software vendor. The experiment prepared it for this moment: where compute capacity is not merely a product, but a strategic commodity.

Oracle is now in the business of signing contracts that resemble long-term oil supply agreements. Its capital expenditure (capex), projected at $35 billion for FY26,5 is staggering. More than half of its annual revenue will be reinvested into GPU infrastructure. But these outlays buy more than machines; they buy relevance. They buy durability. They buy geopolitical positioning.

In that sense, Oracle is less a hyperscaler competitor than a strategic supplier. Like Saudi Aramco has done with oil, Oracle is building the pipelines through which nations and corporations will access compute.

Synopsys: The Silence of Indispensability

While Oracle created a spectacle with its blockbuster results, Synopsys (SNPS) exerts its influence in near silence. Its recent earnings told a more challenging story: sales of $1.74 billion came in below guidance, design intellectual property (IP) revenue was sharply weaker at $428 million versus the $552 million that analysts expected, and EPS fell short. Analysts also had concerns about pro forma noise from the Ansys merger.6

But none of this undermines Synopsys's strategic position. That's because Synopsys owns a large chunk of one of the most irreplaceable bottlenecks in the global economy: electronic design automation (EDA) software.

EDA is the invisible scaffolding of the semiconductor world. It is the set of tools without which chips cannot be designed, tested or manufactured. China can manufacture central processing units (CPUs), GPUs and memory. It can scale fabs. It can write software. But it cannot yet replace EDA. That space remains effectively controlled by three companies—Synopsys, Cadence (CDNS) and Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY)—that collectively own nearly 80% of the EDA market.7

This concentration creates a quiet form of power. Unlike GPUs or fabs, EDA doesn't appear on earnings slides or consumer products. But without it, the semiconductor ecosystem would grind to a halt.

Minerals for Math: The July 2025 Swap

This hidden power became visible in July 2025. Facing a standoff over tariffs and supply chain disruptions, Washington and Beijing struck a deal: the U.S. lifted restrictions on EDA software exports in exchange for China resuming rare earth shipments.8

The symbolism was striking. The U.S. traded access to software tools for access to physical minerals. Commodities for code. Minerals for math.

Each side weaponized its bottleneck. China cut off rare earths, vital inputs for electronics and defense. The U.S. restricted EDA, vital inputs for chip design. Both sides blinked, restoring flows. But the precedent was set: EDA is as strategically vital as rare earths.

Unlike minerals, however, EDA cannot simply be mined. It represents decades of accumulated complexity and institutional knowledge. It is not a natural resource but a cognitive resource. And that makes Synopsys's power even harder to replicate.

Intel: Too Important to Fail

There is a third face of technological power, one that neither Oracle's theater nor Synopsys's silence fully captures: direct state intervention. That role is illustrated by Intel (INTC).

When the U.S. government took a roughly 10% equity stake in Intel, it was more than industrial support. It was partially nationalizing the semiconductor supply chain.9

This stake reframed how investors must view policy. Grants can be withdrawn; credits can be revised. But equity implies alignment of destinies. By owning a share of Intel, the U.S. government has committed not just to supporting the company, but to ensuring its survival, relevance and strategic role in national defense.

Intel thus becomes not only a company, but a policy instrument. Its balance sheet is intertwined with Washington's geopolitical agenda. Its research and development (R&D) roadmap is no longer just a question of market share against Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) or Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC) (TSM); it is a matter of national capacity.

Three Archetypes of Power

Put together, Oracle, Synopsys and Intel represent three archetypes of technological power in the 2020s:

  • Oracle – Visible Scale. Spectacular contracts that reshape narratives and reposition the company as a custodian of compute.
  • Synopsys – Invisible Indispensability. Quiet control over an irreplaceable chokepoint, valuable enough to be traded for rare earth minerals.
  • Intel – Sovereign Alignment. Direct state involvement, with government equity ensuring survival and signaling strategic intent.

This trinity covers the spectrum of how geopolitical power is manifesting in the AI competition.

For Investors: Contrasting Risk Profiles

For investors, these distinctions matter.

Oracle's model is bold but capital-intensive. It is betting on a future where GPUs remain scarce and demand for compute only accelerates. If that thesis holds, its revenue base will compound. If not, the heavy capex may weigh.

Synopsys is subtler. It has faced earnings volatility, but its moat is structural. No advanced chips are made without EDA. Export controls may tighten, but as the July trade showed, the U.S. ultimately views Synopsys as too valuable to restrict for long. It is less a growth story than a perpetual rent story, deeply embedded in the semiconductor lifecycle.

Intel offers a different set of risks entirely. With the U.S. government as a shareholder, downside could be at least partially cushioned. But upside is capped by the reality that Intel is now a strategic utility. Investors must recognize that returns will increasingly be tied to national policy goals, not just market competition.

The Geopolitical Thread

What unites all three is geopolitics. Oracle's contracts make sense only in a world where nations hoard compute as a strategic resource. Synopsys's indispensability only becomes visible in the context of trade wars. Intel's stake only exists because semiconductors are viewed as sovereignty-critical.

The lesson is clear: In the AI era, the boundary between corporate strategy and state policy has dissolved. Companies are no longer merely competing in markets; they are instruments of national power.

Conclusion: The Triangle of Power

In the 20th century, power revolved around oil, steel and finance. In the 21st, it revolves around compute, certain design tools and semiconductors. Oracle has dazzled with its recently reported quarterly bookings. Synopsys disappears into indispensability, bartered like a rare earth mineral. Intel binds itself to the U.S. state, blurring the line between corporation and government.

For investors, this is both an opportunity and a warning. The rewards of scale, scarcity and sovereignty could be immense. But the risks are equally real. Policy is not a tail risk anymore; it is the operating environment.

The real question, then, is not which of these companies is best positioned for the next quarter. It is which form of power—visible scale, invisible indispensability or sovereign alignment—will prove most durable in a world where technology is not just an industry but the frontline of geopolitics.

Footnotes

1 Source: K. Weiss and J. Reynolds, "Oracle Corporation: 1Q26 results – Tectonic shift in business model," Morgan Stanley Research, 9/10/25.

2 Source: D. Gallagher and A. Fitch, "Oracle is the new Nvidia, for better or worse," The Wall Street Journal, 9/10/25.

3 Source: Weiss & Reynolds, 2025.

4 The Stargate Project refers to Oracle's effort to construct mega-scale GPU data centers, effectively a rehearsal for its pivot into becoming a strategic global supplier of compute capacity for AI.

5 FY26 refers to fiscal year 2026 and runs from June 1, 2025, through May 31, 2026, in the case of Oracle.

6 Source: L. Simpson and N. van Putten, "Synopsys Inc.: Surprise weakness in design IP," Morgan Stanley Research, 9/9/25.

7 Source: T. Hale and C. Davies, "Chip software makers say US restrictions on sales to China lifted," Financial Times, 7/3/25.

8 Hale & Davies, 2025.

9 Source: D. Newman, "America's bet on Intel: Why this investment is existential for national security and tech leadership," Futurum Group, 8/26/25.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Samuel Rines, Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Prior to WisdomTree, Samuel was a Managing Director of CORBU, a research firm we struck up a relationship with to deliver model portfolios. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book “After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy ”.

data.title: CRE In An Easing Cycle: Why Long Rates Matter More Than The Fed
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

With the Fed embarking on a new rate-cutting cycle, some investors are left wondering what impact it might have on commercial real estate markets. Historically, the Fed funds rate and 10-year Treasury yields have shown some directional correlation, leading many investors to assume that just as rates rose during the last hiking cycle, they should fall in the next easing cycle. But this assumption is not guaranteed.

Two caveats complicate the picture and have important implications for CRE valuations, financing conditions and investor portfolios.

  1. Correlation is weaker in changes than in levels. While the levels of Fed funds and the 10-year Treasury are positively correlated, the correlation between their day-to-day or cycle-to-cycle moves is much lower.
  2. Long rates are normalizing, not just following the Fed. After a four-decade secular decline, 10-year yields are now closer to their historical norm—averaging 4.35% from 2000 to 2007 and 4.51% between 1990 and 2019, compared with roughly 4.10% today. What was truly unusual was the post-GFC decline to record lows, driven by unprecedented central bank intervention.

Fed funds rate and 10-year Treasury yield

1990–present

What matters most is stability in 10-year Treasury yields

A range of 4.0% to 4.25%—with inflation running near 3%—would create a highly accommodative backdrop for CRE. Real rates are ultimately more relevant than nominal rates given the strong historical correlation between NOI growth and inflation—and that environment implies a real rate of roughly 1% (4% nominal – 3% inflation). For context, real rates have averaged 1.5% from 1997 to today, 1.7% from 1997 to 2019, and 2.9% from 1997 to 2007—periods during which CRE delivered attractive returns.

What does this mean for the next cycle?

In our 2025 mid-year update, Back to Basics, we argued that fundamentals will be a primary driver of total returns versus the post-GFC financially engineered returns that were driven by historically low interest rates. We do not expect significant cap rate compression; instead, total returns will likely come mainly from income and capital growth via NOI. This makes property and market selection critical. For example, data centers stand out for generating some of the highest NOI growth among CRE property types. Our projections for unlevered, asset-level total returns in 2025 are 5–6%, rising toward the low 7% range annualized over the next five years and approaching the historical 10% annualized over the next decade, with certain property types—particularly alternatives like data centers—expected to outperform.

Same property NOI growth by property sector

As of 2Q 2025

Bottom line

For CRE, it’s longer-term interest rates that matter, not the Fed funds rate. After all, the sector typically relies on long-duration financing rather than short-term floating debt. We see little scope for a meaningful decline in 10-year Treasury yields—even if the Fed were to pursue an aggressive easing cycle, which itself is uncertain given sticky inflation. Moreover, if yields were to fall below 4%, that may not be the relief rally some expect. Instead, falling yields could point to a weaker economic backdrop marked by slower growth and wider credit spreads. The real signal to watch is not how fast the Fed cuts, but whether long-term rates decline for the right reasons.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Intel: The Smart Money Is Piling In, Buy On Pullbacks
data.description:
Introduction

Since taking charge of Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) in March 2025, Lip Bu Tan has embarked on an engineering-focused transformation of the semiconductor giant. Now, while we are yet to see any real technological or product progress, Tan & Co. have managed to raise ~$16B in equity capital over the past month from the US Government [$8.9B], SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) [$2B], and Nvidia (NVDA) [$5B].

  • NVIDIA and Intel to Develop AI Infrastructure and Personal Computing Products (Link)
  • SoftBank Group and Intel Corporation Sign $2B Investment Agreement (Link)
  • Intel and Trump Administration Reach Historic Agreement to Accelerate American Technology and Manufacturing Leadership (Link)

Consequently, Mr. Market has bid up INTC stock by ~45% in recent sessions:

Furthermore, Intel is now reportedly in talks with the likes of Apple (AAPL) and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) for additional strategic partnerships and investments.

So, What's Going On? Why Is Everyone Suddenly Interested In Rescuing Intel?

In my view, with the re-shoring of advanced semiconductor manufacturing a key policy initiative for the Trump administration, Intel's position as the only scaled semiconductor manufacturer in the Western Hemisphere is attracting pivotal strategic capital that could spark a genuine business turnaround at the company. To be fair, we saw Intel's strategic value back in 2021 -

Intel's major competitors Nvidia and AMD are fabless semiconductor companies, i.e., they design their chips in-house and get them manufactured at foundries such as Taiwan's TSMC and South Korea's Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF). As you may be aware, TSMC's manufacturing process technology is currently the gold standard in the semiconductor industry. China wants to retake Taiwan (a democratic island situated right off the shore of mainland China) back into its fold. Hence, the current semiconductor supply chain carries massive geopolitical risks.

As of Q2 2021, Intel owns property, plant, and equipment worth ~$58B [largest manufacturing assets among semiconductor manufacturers], and these fabs are located in the Western hemisphere. Intel is probably the only company that can serve as a western alternative to TSMC and Samsung. Therefore, Intel's western fabs are likely to emerge a big advantage over rivals AMD and Nvidia as global organizations look at diversifying their supply chains. Intel recently reported that Amazon (AMZN) and Qualcomm (QCOM) have signed up for their newly created foundry business as early customers. And it is only a matter of time before the likes of Apple, Tesla (TSLA), and many other large corporations start using Intel's US and Europe-based fabs to build their products in the Western hemisphere.

In the interest of national security, the United States needs to have advanced semiconductor manufacturing capabilities on American soil. And Intel is the only company that can rise up to meeting critical infrastructure needs for the US and other western countries. Hence, Intel is an essential semiconductor company for the United States.

Source: Intel Has 3 Big Advantages Over Nvidia And AMD

While Intel's "IDM 2.0 Strategy" has thus far failed to win over major foundry customers, the US Government's 10% ownership stake in Intel validates its strategic importance to national security and supports further capital formation at Intel.

The Nvidia Partnership Is A Game Changer

Despite having no foundry element to it, Nvidia's strategic partnership with Intel to co-develop offerings for the AI and PC markets is set to add ~$100B to Intel's total addressable market.

  • For the AI infrastructure market, Intel will design and manufacture custom x86 CPUs specifically for Nvidia's AI platforms. While Nvidia's ARM-based Grace CPU roadmap remains unchanged, the partnership with Intel will allow Nvidia to offer rack-scale systems addressing the vast x86 ecosystem. According to Jensen Huang (press conference), these custom x86 CPUs represent a $25-50B annual revenue opportunity for Intel.
  • For the PC market, Intel will develop system-on-chip solutions that seamlessly integrate Nvidia's RTX GPU chiplets, utilizing Nvidia's NVLink technology, thereby creating new types of AI PCs that combine the strengths of both Intel's CPUs and Nvidia's GPUs. Over recent years, Intel has been losing market share to AMD, and the hope for this partnership with Nvidia is to arrest the slide and regain market share.

Additionally, in a show of conviction, Nvidia is investing $5B in Intel's common stock -> taking a ~4.3% ownership stake in Intel. With Nvidia investing $100B in OpenAI, the Intel investment may seem like a small hedge of sorts against China-Taiwan geopolitical risk; however, the Nvidia-Intel strategic partnership and investment undoubtedly open the door to future foundry deals between the two American semiconductor giants.

Is Intel Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

With the PC market in recovery mode, Intel's top-line is projected to return to positive y/y growth in 2026, albeit low-single-digit growth:

While execution risk remains elevated, I am optimistic about a real business turnaround for Intel based on recent capital formation and emerging strategic partnerships. Here's my valuation model for INTC stock:

As per these results, Intel is fairly valued at current levels; however, if one were to buy Intel at today's price of ~$34 and hold the stock for five years, they could expect a CAGR return of ~14%, which falls slightly short of TQI's investment hurdle rate of 15%.

While Intel stock is currently a "Hold" under our valuation process, I would be a buyer of INTC if it were to pull back by 5-10% or more from current levels.

Key Takeaway: I rate Intel a "Hold" at $34 per share.

Thanks for reading, and happy investing. Please share your thoughts, concerns, and/or questions in the comments section below.

data.title: U.S. Gold Corp.: An Explorer Shining As Gold Prices Surge, Still Awaiting First Profits
data.description:
The Stock: A U.S. Gold Explorer Making Strides In Wyoming, As The Sector Sees Demand

Earlier in September, BBC reported that "the price of gold has hit a record high as demand for the precious metal remains strong amid global economic uncertainty," so I think it is time to change gears from my usual sectors and cover a gold stock, in the interest of portfolio diversification.

My pick to talk about today is U.S. Gold Corp. (NASDAQ:USAU), whose Seeking Alpha profile says this Nevada-based company "engages in the exploration, evaluation, and development of mineral properties in the United States. It explores for gold, silver, copper, and precious metal deposits."

It is important to point out that this is a firm still in the exploration phase, unlike those larger global firms with mines already producing revenue for many years now.

For those new to this sector, as it has come up in comments, it's important to differentiate between companies that mine/explore gold and gold royalty companies, as they are not the same, nor are those businesses that sell gold coins directly to the public, as they all have a different role to play.

U.S. Gold Corp. made the news in Yahoo! Finance this month said that the firm "has already completed a Preliminary Feasibility Study at the CK Gold Project, an advanced-stage gold and copper project," adding that the company is in the process of supplying power to that project in Wyoming and that U.S. Gold Corp is one of the best American mining stocks to buy now."

To get a taste for what one of its gold facilities looks like, from their investor presentation (pg. 8), below is their CK Gold Project in Wyoming, which is past its preliminary feasibility study phase:

So, is this really one of the best stocks to buy now, and will the gold rush reported in global media like BBC continue? This article aims to unravel some of that further.

The Thesis: A Tepid View Despite Future Prospects

After my research into 7 rating categories, I called this stock a hold, agreeing with the Seeking Alpha quant system, and here is a worksheet summarizing how I got to that rating score and what drove it:

My thesis argues that on the one hand, there are several macro forecasts pointing to continued appreciation in gold prices, and this firm has made strides towards getting its key mining projects towards production mode, all while depending more on equity than debt financing. At the same time, it remains in the exploratory stage and has not proven itself as a revenue or profit generator so far, so investors are depending on those mines to produce the future results expected and the gold price forecasts to come true.

As the Wall St consensus points to a +35% further upside to the share price, I think there is ample justification for a hold/neutral view, while the risks are there to also not make this a buy, and I will show those in the following sections.

The Sector: Early-Stage Mining Carries Added Risk, But Future Gold Prices Look Shiny

I would start off by discussing the overall gold sector, and will be a bit more lengthy in this section due to the added complexity of the gold business.

For my readers who may be experienced investors but somewhat less familiar with this sector, I think it is first important to differentiate between gold explorers/miners vs. gold royalty stream companies, and companies that may sell gold coins directly to consumers.

All or none of the above may fit into someone's overall portfolio strategy, so in the case of U.S. Gold Corp., we will focus on the niche of exploring/mining. However, some investors may not like the risk of waiting on exploration projects to come to fruition at some future date.

In official email commentary from industry expert Max Baecker, president of American Hartford Gold, he shared with me the following this week:

For most investors, buying gold coins directly or through a gold IRA is the purest way to benefit from gold’s upside — you own the metal outright, with no counterparty risk. Mining and royalty companies can offer leverage, but they also face risks from costs, management, or politics. Physical gold provides simplicity, stability, and a direct hedge against uncertainty.

Now that we have some framework for this sector, in any case, the expected future price of gold can have an impact on investing in this sector, so I think investors want to see there is continued future upside in gold prices.

Back in June, for example, JPMorgan (JPM) painted a picture of confidence, saying in their article the following:

Longer term, the 2025 and 2026 outlook for the metal remains bullish. Prices are expected to average $3,675/oz by the fourth quarter of 2025 and climb toward $4,000 by mid-2026.

Central bank and investor demand for gold is set to remain strong, averaging around 710 tonnes a quarter this year.

More recently, on Sept. 17th, Investing.com reported that global financial firm Deutsche Bank (DB) analysts "have raised their forecast for gold prices next year, saying a recent surge in the yellow metal has "further headroom to run" thanks partially to the prospect of easing U.S. interest rates."

Further, data site Statista also forecasted an elevated gold price for 2026:

So, there are multiple sources pointing to an expected continuation of rising gold prices into next year, creating what I would call potential for future upside opportunity for current or past investors, should those forecasts all come true.

I think this also justifies support for gold mining stocks as well, due to the future potential value and return on investment driven by the tailwind of gold prices primarily.

As far as the niche of gold mining, on Aug. 13th, Investopedia reported that UBS (UBS) analysts had their eye on several gold mining stocks:

The analysts said if gold prices remain steady, they see gold mining firms increasing stock buybacks this year, accelerating organic growth projects, and potentially taking part in more merger and acquisition activity.

U.S. Gold Corp. also has to compete for investors with other mining/exploration firms in this space, and as a junior explorer/miner rather than an internationally established one, I think some good peer comparables could include other exploration-stage or early-stage companies trading both on exchanges and OTC: Contango Ore (CTGO), U.S. GoldMining (USGO), Guardian Metal Resources (OTCQX:GMTLF), and Amerigo Resources (OTCQX:ARREF).

I included these as they are said to all be involved in exploration and mining rather than royalty streams or selling coins online, so they make good comparables.

We can also see that this peer group predominantly has a buy or hold rating from the consensus, and no sell ratings at this time, indicating there is lots of support for this niche, and I think it is tied to the bullishness on gold prices:

At the same time, though, the nature of mining for gold also poses environmental and regulatory issues that some investors may consider a potential added risk that some sectors don't have, particularly if mining is irresponsible or unsustainable.

A 2022 paper in the Natl. Library of Medicine stated, for example, that "generally, unsustainable mining has significant adverse impacts on society, the economy, and the environment directly."

U.S. Gold Corp. mitigated some of this regulatory/environmental risk, I think, by being proactive, as it stated in its investor presentation (pg. 13) that it met with "over 300 individuals ranging from the governor to various agencies, interested parties, and individuals."

Based on the multiple factors above combined, I am compelled to be on the low buy range when it comes to sector/industry, as on the one hand, there are clear risks to being an exploratory-phase miner, but on the other hand, the future gold price appreciation potential continues to make a strong case.

Revenue Growth: Progress On Getting Mines Running, But No Proven Revenue So Far

We have talked about the sector overall and why there is cause to be bullish, and cause to be cautious, so now let's consider the company's own specific factors that could impact top-line revenue growth.

The fact is, so far this company has not produced any revenue, only expenses, and this can be seen from the income statement.

This is because it likely does not have revenue-generating mines yet. So, the investment case here is not so much on their past revenue record but future potential.

According to the firm's factsheet, their Keystone Project in Nevada has an "approved plan of operation for targeted drilling," and as far as their CK Gold Project in Wyoming, back in July SA analyst Alberto Abaterusso echoed the point that "according to the updated PFS (preliminary feasibility study), the CK Gold Project now has mineral reserves of 1.672 million ounces of gold equivalent, which support an eight-year mine life."

Further, of the CK Gold Project in Wyoming, the firm's own investor presentation (pg. 3) said that it "offers compelling value with approved development and operating permits advancing towards construction in an advantageous location."

While this may be good news for the future-minded, some caution on mining stocks is justified. For instance, just this week Morningstar wrote of mining stocks that "their cost structure tends to amplify the impact of gold prices on margins, thereby causing gold miners’ stock prices to move more than gold prices. With relatively high fixed costs, their profits are limited when gold prices are low, but rising prices can lead to a significant boost in profit margins."

Many investors may want to see established revenue, and most of this peer comparable group, except for one company, has not really generated revenue growth yet or expected FWD rev growth:

In this case, it seems that USAU has made significant headway towards having mines that eventually begin producing, but has not yet established itself as a revenue producer so far, missing out on this current period of peak gold prices, and dependent on those future gold price forecasts to come true.

For these reasons above, I am leaning more toward the hold range in this category, and although some readers may not agree, I happen to put some more weight on there being at least some proven revenue model to go by.

Profit margins: None Yet, But Future Plans Include Cost Optimization Methods And Clear Return On Investment

Because this firm does not generate revenue yet, there is no data on profit margins (EBITDA and net income margins) since no profits are generated yet.

That is somewhat of a downside factor since there is no real sense yet of what kind of margins this firm can generate over time, in comparison to similar peers.

However, when it comes to U.S. Gold, we can get a sense of what could keep cost efficiency at optimal levels once they start producing. For instance, from the investor presentation (pg. 11) we can learn that the CK Project in Wyoming has an "excellent location, diminishing the need for warehousing and critical spares," adding that there are "OEM equipment supply hubs in Gillette, WY; Denver, CO; and Salt Lake City, UT, all within a 6-hr drive."

I think that could help keep future supply costs under control or optimized.

The firm also expects engineering optimizations and "use of Jameson Cell flotation technology to improve recoveries, capital, and operating costs," according to the same presentation.

I would also highlight from the feasibility data for their key mining project, the CK Project, the following from the investor presentation (pg. 6), which shows that this mine is expected to produce around 110K ounces of gold equivalent per year (AuEq oz/year) with an initial capex of $277MM:

With that said, from the same presentation, I think investors should consider the payback period for this mine as well as NPV and IRR, assuming different scenarios for the price of metals:

So, the above table can give investors a sense of the payback period for this key mine (though not the only one) in this firm's portfolio, and, for instance, the best case scenario will depend on future gold prices being at least $3,000/oz, which is in line with some of those macro forecasts I showed earlier.

So, while this firm has not yet proven itself in competitive profit margins, it does seem to have some strategy for cost optimization, and also has laid out a scenario that will benefit the most if the gold price forecasts come true, and so it deserves at least an upper hold/neutral view in my opinion.

Cash Flow Trends: Negative Operating Cash Flow, But Also No Debt Financing Burden

We have covered the topic of profit margins, cost optimization, and some projections on return on investment for a key mine, so here we will touch on cash flow trends.

For instance, operating cash flow has been negative this entire time, as you can see in the table below, and the firm has yet to generate positive flow:

As there is no cash flow to pay dividends with anyway, it is not a dividend payer, and hence not a great pick for dividend income investors.

However, what the cash flow trends do show is that the company has primarily raised cash via equity financing rather than debt financing, and I think that could lower some downside risk of being over-leveraged.

Here is how that looks in the cash flow statement, showing an extended period of this firm raising cash via issuance of stock:

However, it should be said that over-dependence on equity raising could potentially cause dilution of shares and that is something investors should consider.

On the other hand, raising cash via equity has made it possible to stay afloat. If you look at the cash flow statement, in the 1 year period shown the company has a net change in cash of +$5.7MM:

If the company has $8.2MM in cash as of Sept 1st (investor deck, page 5), then if it continues this trend of +$5.7MM net cash growth it should continue to cover its cash burn by raising equity, and be at around $14MM in cash in a year's time.

Although the argument for dilution can be made, I am more inclined towards equity rather than debt financing while interest rates are still elevated, however with recent Fed decisions, I think a future environment of lower interest rates could be an opportunity to raise cash via lower-cost debt, reducing the need to keep raising equity.

For the reasons above, I think a neutral view would be appropriate at this juncture, as there are several points raised that cause both optimism and concern.

Balance Sheet: Low Leverage Risk As Mostly Financed Via Equity

Moving on to balance sheet risk, as I've pointed out in the prior section, it does not seem that this firm has raised cash through long-term debt financing, and sure enough, there is no long-term debt showing on the balance sheet.

I imagined that this would lead to a debt/equity ratio of nearly 0, and I was right, as you can see in my comparison below. USAU has a nearly 0 D/E and is lower than 2 of its key peers:

Again, I think it raises my rating score somewhat on this company due to having lower leverage risk, which also matters since it is not producing any earnings yet to cover debt payments and interest.

Also, in terms of its quick ratio, to get a sense of whether its liquid assets can cover obligations, USAU has a quick ratio of 11.5, far better than the peers shown, if using a benchmark of at least 1.0 or better, which is my standard benchmark:

While it does not seem to have any recent credit ratings from major agencies like Fitch and Moody's, to add some of their perspective, and that could add some element of risk, I think the data above points to an interesting balance sheet risk profile and moves my needle up into the buy range for this category.

Valuation: An Overvalued P/B For A Company With No Earnings, Yet Some Peers Are Higher

Because this firm has not achieved earnings, we cannot use valuation metrics like FWD P/E since such data does not exist.

However, the price-to-book (P/B) as of Thursday afternoon trading is at 7.99, significantly overvalued vs the sector average of around 2.08.

Based on peer data, it looks like USAU is somewhere in the middle of this peer group in terms of P/B multiples, with two selected peers (early-stage mining firms) considerably more overvalued:

To add an additional angle to this story, it seems not only did this stock's 1 year momentum significantly outperform the S&P500 index, but looking at the price chart below it has been rising as gold prices have been rising too, putting this stock well above its 200-day moving average and near a 5-year high (as of Thursday afternoon price data):

Some may make the argument that the expected future continued rise in gold prices, and the progress made with this firm's mines, justifies this high price.

However, I am going to call it unjustifiably overvalued at this point, as it remains a company with no track record of actually achieving profit margins, operating cash flow growth, or paying out dividends, and it is simply the reality of the business they are in since it takes time and exploration before getting a mine to produce future revenue and profits.

It remains undervalued to some peers, though, and this helps its cause, so for this reason I am going with hold in this case.

Future Price Target: Consensus Calls for +35% Upside

Because there is no FWD P/E multiple to use, and the consensus calls for negative EPS in both fiscal 2026 and 2027, in lieu of my usual table of price targets based on those metrics, I think we can simply take a look at the avg consensus target from Wall St., as shown in Seeking Alpha:

In this case, the consensus expects a nearly +35% upside to the latest share price, with a target price of $21.06. This would actually significantly break through the 5-year high I showed in the chart earlier, and I think it is being driven by the bullish expectations for gold prices mostly.

Nevertheless, I think investors do pay attention to such price targets, and a +35% upside forecast should justify some more weight on the buy side of my rating table rather than holding.

Challenge To My Rating: Impacts On Gold Prices

A challenge to my neutral rating could come, of course, from gold prices not living up to forecasts but actually declining, making my cautious optimism overly positive, particularly since a lot of this business seems to depend on macro-level gold and metal prices (copper, silver, etc.) as this firm is also involved in other metals, like some of its peers.

As I mentioned earlier, like what Max Baecker of American Hartford Gold said, investors like gold as a hedge against uncertainty, hence spiking demand for it and therefore sending its price up too, and we have seen over the last year the market volatility triggered in part by uncertainty over tariff policy and its potential impact.

An extended period of stability could reverse this environment, of course, especially if other investments begin looking much more attractive than holding gold, such as oil or non-gold sector stocks. For instance, in high-interest environments where money market funds saw yields topping 4%, and I got into some of them myself, I think it makes those types of investments more attractive than a stock yielding 0% in dividends. In future low-interest rate environments, real estate or REIT stocks may be more attractive than gold due to lower cost of capital and proven rental income.

So, simply betting that gold will keep going up forever and providing a stable asset is not prudent, and history proves this.

According to the site Trading Economics, for instance, we can see that historically gold saw much lower prices for an extended period between 2016- 2020, and there is no telling whether it can also drop to that level again if demand drops.

So, I think to mitigate such risk, the price of gold should only be one factor for investors, but they should also consider a company's fundamentals and whether the firm proves itself as a continuing entity even if future gold prices drop.

I think what is good about USAU is that it showed, for example, with its CK Project mine, what kind of a return on investment it could see under different gold price scenarios.

Conclusion: Agree With Quant System

This time, though not always, I am agreeing with Seeking Alpha's quant system, which says to hold on to this stock.

To conclude, I have shown what could drive future upside, and it is mostly due to low leverage risk, mines making progress, and continued rise of gold prices.

I also have shown that this is a firm without a proven track record of revenue and profits, or positive operating cash flow and dividends, so there remains some element of risk there to consider since investors are betting on future income potential.

data.title: Oklo: Mini-Reactor Opportunity At Full Price
data.description:
Oklo (NYSE:OKLO) is a rising nuclear power play. Having covered it before and seeing its stock rally through 2025, I wanted to cover it one more time. Needless to say, I've found the stock climbs faster than the fundamentals, and it's the first time I've found myself saying "fully valued" when it comes to a stock.

Recap Of Previous Thoughts

I first covered OKLO in August last year. The stock was still below $8 at the time, and it didn't have quite as much attention. I was impressed with the possibility that nuclear mini-reactors offered.

Old school nuclear typically required enough size to generate electricity. Nuclear's failure to replace fossil fuels in the quest for green energy largely stems from other environmental and health concerns, such as those resulting from meltdowns. Oklo's designs allow for a model that is mobile and deployable outside of major electrical grids, without the same meltdown risks that come with large quantities of nuclear material.

As this would have a near-term use case in the development of data centers, as well as anything that requires powering remote facilities and projects, it was easy to see Oklo's value-add.

The problem at the time was the lack of sales, the fact that production facilities needed to be developed, and the need for financing in the meantime, which posed dilutive risks in spite of the upside.

When I covered it again in May, the pipeline of customer interest had grown, to about 14 GW of demand, from 1.4 GW during my initial coverage. I noted that, depending on our assumptions of their eventual net margins, such a pipeline suggested $1.2B in future earnings, not without some likelihood for more share dilution.

It was a thesis that required a lot of patience, with shareholder returns leaning heavily toward a period of 5 - 10 years. It seemed like OKLO could double (then priced at $28) in a decade, but this depended on a very general outlook.

Updates and Current Status

As Oklo continued to raise capital, it bolstered its liquid position significantly, but that's about all that's occurred in 2025 so far.

Liquidity grew by almost $300M to about $535M. They even got a $148.5M portfolio of debt securities to give them some interest income, mitigating cash burn by a few million. Note, however, that very little property and equipment is on the balance sheet at all. Their acquisition of Atomic Alchemy added more goodwill to the balance sheet than it did any hard assets.

Most of the outflow is just in the operating expenses. Capex has been light, and once development of their facilities begins, that will hasten the burn.

On that note, Oklo announced breaking ground on their Aurora facility just a few days ago. This is their first production facility, and it stands to reason that a slight increase in capex will show in Q3's results and into Q4 thereafter.

More progress is being made on the preparatory side. In July, they completed an assessment in their licensing process. They've announced partnerships with Vertiv (VRT) and with Liberty Energy (LBRT). They've also been selected by the Department of Energy for a reactor pilot program.

For the most part, it's a continuation of the previous plan, with things more or less occurring on schedule or at their natural pace.

Pondering the Valuation

The stock price has come a long way even since my last coverage in May, when it was $28.

The share price has hovered between $130 and $140 recently, putting the market cap over $20B. In previous cases where the stock price increased, it usually followed earnings calls in which the potential customer pipeline increased. As I mentioned before, it grew from a 1.4GW to 14GW by the end of Q4 2024.

That pipeline, based only on letters of interest, was not changed at the end of Q2, and none of the recent new releases by the company necessarily indicate if this represents an expansion of the pipeline.

Really, what we are seeing is a market willing to pay at least $20B for a company that was just under $4B, despite no revenue and mostly the same information as before. The only thing that I think has changed is general awareness of the market about OKLO and a desire to be early.

In the grand scheme of its life, can I see OKLO being a $20B company? Sure, because this is a revolutionary product with use cases beyond the data center opportunity. We just have to wait about two years for their first sales, and then we have to wait however many more years after that for earnings to get into the billions. In my last thesis, I worried that it looked early even for a few hundred million in earnings.

OKLO doesn't need to crash to be a bad Buy right now. One only needs to be so early that would be pretty flat for years. A lot of stocks are fairly valued.

My worry is that OKLO seems to be fully valued right now.

Conclusion

With the stock having come so far since I first covered it, I wanted to address it one more time, to remark upon the gargantuan shift in valuation, despite marginal changes in the fundamentals. I think I will wait until it actually has some sales on its core reactor product before I reassess. OKLO doesn't seem doomed in the long run, and I've never seen a reason to bet against it. In the interest of getting meaningful returns, buyers should beware. I maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: FBL: Strategic Tool For Active Traders Looking To Amplify Meta Gains
data.description:
Introduction

The GraniteShares 2x Long META Daily ETF (NASDAQ:FBL) is a leveraged fund that aims to provide investors with 200% of the daily percentage change of the underlying company, Meta. This fund is best for active traders who are aware of the risks of investing in levered funds, and who can monitor the market and Meta news to stay on top of any market activity. Please see the below image for some key facts, from the prospectus.

Additionally, the daily volume is 568K, which is strong for an ETF. Its average volume is 524K, which also signifies strong liquidity.

We like Meta as a company and in the below section will review some recent news and announcements that could be catalysts for trading FBL.

Underlying Company News

Meta reveals new smart glasses

This reveal, ahead of their annual Connect show, is a prime example of something that can move Meta stock, and therefore be a good catalyst to trade FBL with. Meta's new virtual reality product is an unreleased pair of RayBan sunglasses that come with a display and a wristband to control the user interface. This technology is possible through surface electromyography, which allows the device to interpret hand signals that can control the platform. An announcement like this, which can often drive retail hype and speculation, is a great example of a catalyst to trade FBL with. Ahead of their upcoming Connect event, which might reveal even more new products, partnerships, or news, FBL is a strong way for active traders to get exposure to Meta stock movement.

Meta's AI group is innovating and being more efficient

There was a recent release of a new, compact AI model from Meta's AI labs, called the MobileLLM R1, which is an edge reasoning model with less than 1 billion parameters, so very small by relative standards, but performs 2-5x better than comparable sized models from peers. See the below image that highlights this model's performance.

This model, according to Meta, is able to match Alibaba's Qwen3 0.6B parameter model with 8.6x less data, which is very impressive. It is also powered by 22 transformers, which are critical to the model being able to process prompts and quickly return a response. These transformers are able to take prompts, turn them into complex mathematical equations, draw relationships and conclusions, and provide an answer to the user. Meta's new model shows that the firm can innovate well in AI, and that their AI investments in hiring are starting to pay off.

Meta is continuing to innovate through partnerships in key areas like cybersecurity

The firm recently announced a new benchmarking tool using AI for enterprise cybersecurity teams, in a partnership with CrowdStrike. This platform will help security teams measure and benchmark their systems, to evaluate how prepared it may be for a real world cyber attack. This sort of testing and iterative approach to cybersecurity is critical to avoid any major hack, and these are 2 leading companies at the forefront of advanced computing and cybersecurity. We believe this is the sort of news that could move the stock as well. Further, if this is just the start of Meta getting into cybersecurity, that could spell another possibly lucrative business for the firm. We are bullish on this announcement, and believe that this is the exact type of catalyst that could be a buying opportunity on a fund like FBL.

Peer ETFs

There are 2 other ETFs that we looked at for comparison purposes for FBL. See the chart below. All figures are taken from their pages on Yahoo Finance.

ETF

Expense Ratio

YTD Performance

FBL - 2x

1.15%

37.17%

METU - 2x

1.06%

30.88%
